title: Effective Gilbert damping in the stochastic Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert equation
authors: Mexx. E. Y. Regout, Bertrand Dupé, Matthieu J. Verstraete
abstract: Quasi particle based (e.g. Boltzmann equation) studies of spin wave transport often assume that their scattering rates follow the simple form $η=αω$, with the Gilbert damping $α$ and frequency $ω$. In this work, we examine the effective damping $α_{eff,T}=η/ω$ observed in atomistic spin dynamics, when temperature and spin wave interactions are introduced for a 1D spin chain. We extract the dynamical correlation functions from spin trajectories propagated using the stochastic Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert equation, and fit the dynamical structure factor, yielding the dispersion and scattering rates for a wide range of temperatures. The resulting effective damping can be very different from the initially constant Gilbert value. It exhibits a temperature and crystal momentum scaling which we explain based on interactions with the Gilbert bath and spin wave scattering by changes in local magnetic order.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the effective Gilbert damping in a 1D spin chain is not a constant but changes significantly with temperature and momentum. The researchers show that increased spin wave interactions with local magnetic order at higher temperatures lead to enhanced, momentum-dependent damping.
main_problem: Investigating how temperature and spin wave interactions cause the effective Gilbert damping to deviate from its initial constant value in a 1D spin chain.
main_result: The effective damping increases with temperature and develops momentum dependence due to a transition from quadratic to linear scattering scaling, driven by spin wave interactions with local magnetic order.
method: Atomistic spin dynamics simulations using the stochastic Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert equation, with spectral analysis via fitting the dynamical structure factor using Lorentzian and Voigt profiles.
model_or_system: A 1D classical Heisenberg spin chain with nearest-neighbor exchange interaction and an external magnetic field, modeled using the stochastic Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert (sLLG) equation.
key_observables: Spin wave dispersion, scattering rates, dynamical structure factor, and effective Gilbert damping.
important_parameters: Reduced temperature (T*), exchange interaction (J1), Gilbert damping (alpha), and local order parameter.
main_assumptions: The use of a linearized LLG regime assumes small fluctuations around the quantization axis, and the quasi-particle approximation is used for the scattering analysis.
figures_summary: Figures show the dynamical structure factor at different temperatures, the temperature-dependent softening of spin wave dispersion, the evolution of scattering rates from quadratic to linear scaling, and the emergence of momentum-dependent effective damping.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of temperature-dependent damping, describes the 1D Heisenberg model and sLLG method, presents results on dispersion softening and scattering rate scaling, and concludes with an analysis of the effective damping evolution and anti-magnon dynamics.
why_interesting: The study explores how a thermal bath and many-body interactions modify the intrinsic dissipation parameters of a system, which is highly relevant to understanding decoherence and dissipation in open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Averaged dynamical structure factor ¯S(q, Ω) at a temperature of T = 10−3 T ∗. (a) The dispersion increases from q = 0.0 which corresponds to a homogeneous excitation to q = 0.5 (2π/a) which has a periodicity of two unit cell. The presence of a gap at q = 0.0 is due to the external magnetic field (Bz). (b) Shows three examples of individual momentum mode q = 0.0, q = 0.25 (2π/a) and q = 0.5 (2π/a) in green, orange and blue, respectively. Each q mode can be analyzed based on the dynamical structure factor ¯S(q, Ω) from which the peak position ω(q) and line-width η(q) can be determined which is represented by the horizontal and vertical dashed lines respectively.

FIG. 2. Averaged dynamical structure factor ¯S(q, Ω) for dif- ferent temperatures with respect to T ∗. (a) Shows the low temperature limit where the cosine dispersion and broaden- ing appear. (b)-(c) Shows, with increasing temperature, the reduction of the dispersion, broadening of the peaks and emer- gence of intensity around and below zero frequency. Note that, at each q, the ¯S(q, Ω) are normalized to a maximum intensity of one, to highlight relative differences between q modes.

FIG. 3. Averaged dynamical structure factor ¯S(qZB, Ω), at T = 0.5 T ∗and qZB = 0.5 (2π/a), with different fitting ap- proaches. (a) The Lorentzian fit is obtained from the full fre- quency range (solid green curve) and the windowed fit (dashed green curve). The Voigt fit is applied in the windowed region (solid red curve). (b) Comparison of the same Lorentzian and Voigt fits on a logarithmic scale. We see that the unwindowed Lorentzian fit does not fit very well to either side of the peak. With the application of the window we see that the Lorentzian fit is improved but the windowed Voigt fits the right side of the peak more accurately than the windowed Lorentzian.

FIG. 4. Temperature dependent dispersions ℏ¯ωT (q) obtained from the Voigt fits. We observe the expected dispersion for T = 10−3 T ∗(blue curve) which coincides with Eq. 3. For higher temperatures we see the progressive softening of the dispersion. The data points represent the averaged fitted peak positions by Eq. A2. The error-bars are within the point size. The solid lines are the fit results of ℏωT (q) = (1+α2)−1·[4JT · (1−cos(qa))+µmBz] to ℏ¯ωT (q), where JT is the fit parameter which is shown in the inset as points (solid lines are a guide to the eye). The error-bars in the inset, from lmfit, are within the point size.

FIG. 5. Averaged local magnetization |M(Nc, T)| for differ- ent reduced temperatures T ∗and cluster size Nc. The fitted ∆T lies in between the three (Nc = 3) and four (Nc = 4) spin clusters except for the lowest temperature where it is below both curves. For increasing temperatures we observe a transition in interaction length from next nearest neighbor (Nc = 4) to nearest neighbor (Nc = 3).
